Looking at description at 0.1.x [version](https://docs.rs/time/0.1.44/time/fn.now.html), it says `Returns the current time in the local timezone`, so i guess it is.
From 0.3.6 (https://github.com/time-rs/time/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md#036-2022-01-20) `now_local` can be used again on more platforms.

body: Does `#[inline]` have any meaning here when the method is generic anyway?
author: Kobzol

body: I'm not sure either! Other methods are all annotated `#[inline]` so I decided to follow suit.
author: ksqsf

body: It doesn't really matter for generic functions indeed. Maybe it would in a future implementation of rustc, but it's hard to imagine. We could possibly make a lint for it if we decide `#[inline]` will never have an effect on generic functions.
author: m-ou-se

body: Isn't eslint complaining when you use backticks without `${}` content?
author: GuillaumeGomez

body: Nope! I wouldn't expect it to. Backticks do a bunch of things besides template expansion. They also allow multiline strings, and serve as a different delimiter so you don't have to backslash double quotes (`\"`)
author: jsha

body: I think backticks would be better here as well.
author: GuillaumeGomez

body: We wanted to make this change in bigger PR but I guess it's fine... Don't forget to update the comment just above please.
author: GuillaumeGomez

body:
I actually think a giant PR adding ES6 features wherever possible, and updating the linter at the same time would be a bad idea. The way we should do it is like I'm doing here:

 - update the linter to accept es6 as a standalone commit
 - start using es6 features where they are useful
 - do a series of standalone commits that do one of two things:
   - adopt an ES6 feature where it is useful (e.g. const everything that can be consted), or
   - migrate a discrete chunk of code to using various useful ES6 features.
author: jsha

body: Like I said, it's fine. :wink:
author: GuillaumeGomez

body: You can remove the "conservative estimate", which only made sense with `min_size`.
author: eddyb

body: r=me with that comment tweaked to not say "conservative estimate"
author: eddyb

body: I suppose I had hoped to be able to predict certain sizes well enough to have a lot of single-byte LEB128 encodings, but in retrospect it doesn't seem doable without a bunch of custom derives. In most cases, distances under 128 wouldn't make a difference anyway, and I suspect that's most of what `min_size` could ever handle (outside of `Lazy<[T]>`, I suppose).
author: eddyb

author: dtolnay

body:
```suggestion
  type on the C side.][62514] Please see [the documentation][box-memory-layout] for more information,
  including the important caveat about preferring to avoid `Box<T>` in Rust signatures for functions defined in C.
```

From the documentation:

> Important. At least at present, you should avoid using `Box<T>` types for functions that are defined in C but invoked from Rust. In those cases, you should directly mirror the C types as closely as possible. Using types like `Box<T>` where the C definition is just using `T*` can lead to undefined behavior, as described in rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines#198.

Seems good to call out directly, I think.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: That's why I thought it necessary to at least include the link to the docs. Was not sure if including the warning would make the bullet point too big. Committed the suggestion
author: nico-abram

body:
```suggestion
- [You can now define a Rust `extern "C"` function with `Box<T>` and use `T*` as the corresponding
  type on the C side.][62514] Please see [the documentation][box-memory-layout] for more information,
```
author: camelid

author: fmease

body:
```suggestion
                                    if code.chars().count() == 1 {
```

However that is not very performant if the string literal is large (it keeps iterating over the string even if we already know it's larger than one). So maybe:

```suggestion
                                    if code.chars().nth(1).is_none() {
```
author: fmease

body:
Oh no, I think my suggestion also fires for empty string literals `""`, sorry :worried:! I think it's too late to change in this PR since it's already approved.

```suggestion
                                    if code.chars().nth(1).is_none() && !code.is_empty() {
```

Hmm that code is not that self-explanatory.
